claims.claim: Death penalty moratorium covers 1,337 inmates since 2018
claims.source: Amnesty International, public statement, 4 July 2025, cites Malaysian parliamentary written answer of 4 Oct 2022 for 1,337 and states official figures as of 22 Jan 2025 were 140: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf; Amnesty 2018 report notes Malaysia announced a moratorium in 2018: https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2019/04/death-penalty-facts-and-figures-2018/
claims.correction: The moratorium began in 2018, but 1,337 was a pre-reform death-row figure cited for 2022, not the current number covered. By 22 January 2025, official figures cited by Amnesty put Malaysia's death-row population at 140.
claims.stale_check: If written before the 2023-2025 resentencing process, the 1,337 figure may have reflected the earlier death-row population; it is stale for 2025/2026 use.

claims.claim: Malaysia's moratorium on executions, in place since 2018
claims.source: Amnesty International, Death Penalty 2018 Facts and Figures, published 10 Apr 2019, states Malaysia announced a moratorium on executions in 2018: https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2019/04/death-penalty-facts-and-figures-2018/
claims.stale_check: No contrary recent change found; Malaysia recorded 0 executions in 2024 in Amnesty's 2024 report.

claims.claim: applies to 1,337 death row inmates as Parliament debates the Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 846, gazetted 16 Jun 2023: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf; Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf
claims.correction: Act 846 was passed and gazetted in 2023, not merely being debated now. 1,337 was a pre-reform death-row figure, not the current number after resentencing.
claims.stale_check: The framing appears stale after Act 846 took effect on 4 July 2023 and the Federal Court review concluded on 29 October 2024.

claims.claim: 1,337 inmates await resentencing after 2023 mandatory death penalty abolition
claims.source: Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025, lines on 936 reviewed and 140 remaining as of 22 Jan 2025: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf
claims.correction: Before repeal, 1,337 people were under sentence of death. Under Act 847, 936 death-row prisoners who had exhausted ordinary appeals were reviewed by 29 October 2024; the death-row population was reported at 140 as of 22 January 2025.
claims.stale_check: Stale/incorrect after the 2024 completion of Federal Court temporary review.

claims.claim: Courts must individually review each case under new sentencing guidelines
claims.source: UN Malaysia national report for UPR, A/HRC/WG.6/45/MYS/1, states Act 847 provides temporary Federal Court jurisdiction to review death and natural-life sentences: https://documents.un.org/api/symbol/access?l=ru&s=A%2FHRC%2FWG.6%2F45%2FMYS%2F1&t=pdf; Act 846 gazette: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf
claims.correction: The review mechanism is real, but 'each case' and 'new sentencing guidelines' overstate the source. Act 847 created a temporary review jurisdiction; the analysis should specify eligible cases/applications and avoid implying a published comprehensive guideline unless sourced.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that validates the broader wording.

claims.claim: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 replaced 11 capital offences
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 846, gazetted 16 Jun 2023: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf; Bernama, 3 Apr 2023, reporting Ramkarpal's tabling statement on 11 offences: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2178595
claims.correction: More precise wording: Act 846 abolished mandatory death penalty provisions affecting 11 mandatory-death offences and amended seven statutes; it did not abolish or replace all capital offences, and the death penalty remains available for some offences.
claims.stale_check: No later change found altering Act 846's basic scope.

claims.claim: Judges now have discretion to impose life imprisonment or 30-40 year terms
claims.source: Laws of Malaysia Act 846, long title and amendments, gazetted 16 Jun 2023: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf; Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf
claims.correction: Act 846 repealed mandatory death sentences and imprisonment for natural life. For many offences the alternative is death or imprisonment for 30 to 40 years, often with whipping where applicable. 'Life imprisonment' is not an accurate general substitute in this wording.
claims.stale_check: No later change found making this wording accurate.

claims.claim: Amnesty International recorded 1,342 global executions in 2024 across 16 nations
claims.source: Amnesty International, Death Sentences and Executions 2024, Apr 2025, records 1,518 executions in 15 countries: https://www.amnesty.sk/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Amnesty-Death-Sentences-and-Executions-2024-v2-WEB-1.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with: Amnesty International recorded 1,518 executions in 2024 across 15 countries. Amnesty notes China figures are excluded from the published total because they are state secrets.
claims.stale_check: No; this is contradicted by Amnesty's 2024 report.

claims.claim: Malaysia joined 106 countries that have abolished mandatory death sentences
claims.source: Amnesty International, Death Penalty 2018 Facts and Figures, states 106 countries had abolished the death penalty in law for all crimes by end-2018: https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2019/04/death-penalty-facts-and-figures-2018/; Act 846 gazette: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf
claims.correction: The 106 figure refers to countries that had abolished the death penalty for all crimes by end-2018, not countries abolishing mandatory death sentences. Malaysia abolished the mandatory death penalty in 2023 but retained capital punishment.
claims.stale_check: The claim appears to mix a 2018 global abolition figure with Malaysia's 2023 mandatory-death reform.

claims.claim: 78% of death row inmates were convicted of drug offences under DDA 1952
claims.source: Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025, says 40 of 140 people under sentence of death as of 22 Jan 2025 were convicted of drug-related offences: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf; Death Penalty Project, 3 Apr 2023, reported 67.5% of death row were convicted of drug trafficking as of Feb 2023: https://deathpenaltyproject.org/malaysia-set-to-abolish-the-mandatory-death-penalty/; Laws of Malaysia Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 via MOH: https://pharmacy.moh.gov.my/sites/default/files/document-upload/dangerous-drugs-act-1952-act-234_2.pdf
claims.correction: Use a dated figure. Available sources show about 67.5% as of February 2023, 70% in December 2019, and 40 of 140 as of 22 January 2025. I found no primary source supporting 78%.
claims.stale_check: If from an older dataset, the figure may be stale; it is not valid after the 2024-2025 resentencing reductions.

claims.claim: Lower-income communities disproportionately represented on death row
claims.source: Lucy Harry, 'Rethinking the Relationship between Women, Crime and Economic Factors: The Case-Study of Women Sentenced to Death for Drug Trafficking in Malaysia', Laws 2021, peer-reviewed: https://www.mdpi.com/2075-471X/10/1/9; Monash/ADPAN report on drug offences and death penalty, 2020: https://www.monash.edu/__data/assets/pdf_file/0011/2213867/MU_ADPAN_report_v6.pdf
claims.correction: There is academic and NGO evidence that economically precarious foreign national women and drug couriers are vulnerable in Malaysia's death-penalty system, but I did not find a primary statistical source proving the broad claim that lower-income communities as a whole are disproportionately represented on death row.
claims.stale_check: The post-2023 resentencing process substantially changed death-row composition, so pre-reform socioeconomic findings should be dated and caveated.

claims.claim: Reform passed, but 1,337 people still wait years for resentencing hearings
claims.source: Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf; Amnesty International, Death Sentences and Executions 2024: https://www.amnesty.sk/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Amnesty-Death-Sentences-and-Executions-2024-v2-WEB-1.pdf
claims.correction: By 29 October 2024, the Federal Court had concluded its temporary review process for 936 death-row applicants. By 22 January 2025, official figures cited by Amnesty put the total death-row population at 140, not 1,337 awaiting hearings.
claims.stale_check: This was overtaken by the 2024 completion of the temporary review process.

claims.claim: Only 186 of 1,337 cases resentenced by end of 2025 due to court backlog
claims.source: Amnesty International public statement, 4 Jul 2025, reports 936 reviewed by 29 Oct 2024 and at least 1,016 commutations by Federal Court and Court of Appeal by end-2024: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf
claims.correction: The number 186 is contradicted by available figures. Amnesty reports 854 commutations under the temporary Federal Court process, 46 further Federal Court commutations under ordinary review, and 116 Court of Appeal commutations in 2024, totalling at least 1,016 commutations by end-2024. The 'due to court backlog' attribution is also unsupported.
claims.stale_check: No; by mid-2025 the available data already contradicted this claim.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Death penalty moratorium covers 1,337 inmates since 2018
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Conflates the 2018 moratorium with a 2022/pre-reform death-row figure and implies current coverage of 1,337 despite later official figures showing 140 as of 22 January 2025.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Amnesty International public statement citing Malaysian parliamentary written answers: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Only 186 of 1,337 cases resentenced by end of 2025 due to court backlog
cardinal_sin_flags.why: I found no primary source for 186 or for the causal claim of court backlog; available figures show far more cases reviewed/commuted by end-2024.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Amnesty International public statement citing Parliament/Federal Court Registrar figures: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 1,337 inmates await resentencing after 2023 mandatory death penalty abolition
cardinal_sin_flags.why: 1,337 was the pre-reform death-row population, not the number still awaiting resentencing after reforms; the temporary Federal Court review had concluded by 29 October 2024.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 78% of death row inmates were convicted of drug offences under DDA 1952
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source found for 78%; identified figures differ materially: 67.5% in Feb 2023 reporting, 70% in Dec 2019 reporting, and 40 of 140 as of Jan 2025.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Death Penalty Project, 3 Apr 2023: https://deathpenaltyproject.org/malaysia-set-to-abolish-the-mandatory-death-penalty/; Amnesty public statement: https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Courts must individually review each case under new sentencing guidelines
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Act 847 created a temporary Federal Court review jurisdiction for eligible cases; the wording implies universal review and formal new sentencing guidelines that were not verified.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysia joined 106 countries that have abolished mandatory death sentences
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The 106 figure refers to countries abolishing the death penalty for all crimes by end-2018, not mandatory death sentences. Malaysia retained the death penalty after Act 846.

omitted_facts: Act 846 was gazetted on 16 June 2023 and came into force on 4 July 2023; source: Laws of Malaysia Act 846 https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/1826840_BI/Akta%20846%20BI.pdf and Bernama citing gazette https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2203204

Act 847 came into force on 12 September 2023 and created temporary Federal Court review jurisdiction; source: Malaysian Bar circular reproducing ministerial statement https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/cms/upload_files/document/Circular%20No%20003-2023LW.pdf

The Federal Court concluded the temporary death-sentence review on 29 October 2024 after reviewing 936 people on death row; source: Amnesty public statement citing February 2025 parliamentary answer https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf

By 22 January 2025, official figures cited by Amnesty indicated 140 people remained under sentence of death in Malaysia, including 40 for drug-related offences; source: Amnesty public statement https://www.amnesty.org/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ACT5095572025ENGLISH.pdf

Amnesty recorded 1,518 executions in 2024 in 15 countries, not 1,342 in 16; source: Amnesty Death Sentences and Executions 2024 https://www.amnesty.sk/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Amnesty-Death-Sentences-and-Executions-2024-v2-WEB-1.pdf

Malaysia recorded 0 executions and 24 new death sentences in 2024; source: Amnesty Death Sentences and Executions 2024 https://www.amnesty.sk/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Amnesty-Death-Sentences-and-Executions-2024-v2-WEB-1.pdf
source_assessment: The original analysis lists no sources, so its source diversity is effectively zero. The claims require legal texts, parliamentary answers, prison/court statistics, and human-rights monitoring data, but none are cited. The published cards also omit government/court sources, defence or victims' perspectives, prison administration data, legal-aid perspectives, and current post-resentencing figures. For verification I found the strongest sources to be Act 846 from the Laws of Malaysia portal, UN/Malaysia reporting on Act 847, Bernama reports quoting gazette or ministers, Amnesty reports citing parliamentary and Federal Court figures, and peer-reviewed academic work on socioeconomic vulnerability.
overall_assessment: The analysis is not reliable as published. It correctly identifies the 2018 moratorium and the existence of the 2023 mandatory death penalty reforms, but several central numbers are wrong or stale. The largest failures are the repeated use of 1,337 as if it were the current resentencing backlog, the unsupported 186 resentenced figure, the incorrect global execution total, and the unsupported 78% drug-offence figure. The piece should be substantially corrected before publication, with dated figures and primary legal/statistical sources added.
